title: Optimizing Loudspeaker Performance Through Z-Domain Vector Fitting and Frequency-Warped Signal Processing
authors: Edgar B. Righi, Flavio A. M. Cipparrone, Phillip M. S. Burt
publish: IEEE Transactions on Audio, Speech and Language Processing
abstract: Digital equalization is essential for correcting loudspeaker frequency responses in high-fidelity audio reproduction. Although infinite impulse response (IIR) filters are computationally efficient, conventional high-order design methods often lack perceptual relevance and may suffer from numerical instability. Warped IIR (WIIR) filters offer improved perceptual alignment, but robust high-order design remains challenging. This paper proposes a loudspeaker equalization methodology based on WIIR filters derived using the z-domain Vector Fitting (VFz) algorithm operating directly in a warped discrete-frequency domain. By integrating frequency warping into the fitting process, the proposed approach enables perceptually aligned modeling while preserving the numerical robustness and flexibility of VFz. The approach is validated through the design and real-time implementation of a high-order loudspeaker equalizer on a digital signal processor. Experimental results demonstrate sub-<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\text{0.1}\,\text{dB}$</tex-math></inline-formula> modeling accuracy and effective equalization performance across the audible range, providing an efficient solution for high-precision loudspeaker equalization in audio systems.

title: Parametric Sound Field Interpolation for Scene-based Navigable Immersive Audio
authors: Orhun Olgun, Ibrahim Ethem Deveci, Hüseyin Hachabiboğlu
publish: IEEE Transactions on Audio, Speech and Language Processing
abstract: Modern eXtended Reality (XR) systems are capable of tracking their users' positions within a navigable volume. This opens up the possibility of more realistic interactions with the content not only via head rotations, but also via locomotion. Such capability necessitates the design of audio systems that can generate a perceptually veridical rendering of the sound field at every position which the user can navigate to. While such interactivity can be provided by object-based audio (OBA) renderers such as in 3D computer games and VR applications, the new generation of volumetric visual content requires volumetric capture and rendering of the entire captured scenes, without explicitly identifying/extracting the audio objects therein. This article presents a parametric sound field interpolation method via multi-point sparse plane wave decomposition (SPWD) in the spherical harmonic domain. The proposed approach relies on higher-order Ambisonics (HOA) recordings of sound fields captured at multiple positions. The sound field is separated into direct and diffuse components that are interpolated parametrically and linearly, respectively. The proposed approach is compared with linear interpolation objectively and subjectively to demonstrate its utility.

title: From Uncertainty to Decision: Enhancing Goal-Oriented Dialogue Planning under Hesitation
authors: Xiaoyu Du, Shuangqing Li, Yanxin Zhang, Boying Li, Tailai Zhou, Yibing Chen, Lizi Liao, Jinhui Tang
publish: IEEE Transactions on Audio, Speech and Language Processing
abstract: Dialogue planning is a critical task in proactive dialogue systems, where the main challenge lies in formulating effective, goal-oriented strategies, such as those required in sales negotiation or emotional support. Large Language Models, while powerful at generating contextually appropriate responses, remain limited in their capacity for long-term planning. To address this gap, recent approaches have drawn inspiration from cognitive theories of decision-making, combining a fast, reactive policy network with a slower, more deliberative planning mechanism such as Monte Carlo Tree Search (MCTS). This paradigm has shown promise in enhancing the strategic capabilities of dialogue agents. However, MCTS-based planners face a critical limitation: when the policy network assigns nearly identical scores to multiple actions, exploration becomes unfocused, leading to inefficient simulation and reinforcement of suboptimal strategies. We introduce a success-aware action selection mechanism with two key components: a) a scoring bonus that amplifies actions empirically associated with successful dialogue outcomes during simulations, making them more distinguishable and guiding exploration toward higher-value strategies; and b) a balancing scheme that integrates offline pre-training with MCTS-guided online fine-tuning, jointly improving planning efficiency and strategy quality. Experiments on multiple dialogue tasks demonstrate consistent improvements in both success rate and planning efficiency over representative baselines. Our code is available at <uri xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">https://github.com/ChloeLee-lsq/SAAS.git</uri>.

title: Transformer based Encoder Representation for Audio Anti-spoofing: Separability and Alignment
authors: Yu-Li Ding, Hung-Hsu Tsai, Yin-Tzer Shih
publish: IEEE Transactions on Audio, Speech and Language Processing
abstract: This paper proposes a Modified Audio Anti-Spoofing method based on a pretrained transformer and the strategy of separability and alignment, which is called MAAS-SA hereafter. Traditional schemes suffer from a challenge on generalizations for cross multiple languages because their models are solely trained by a single language dataset. The MAAS-SA method utilizes three distinct language datasets to overcome the challenge mentioned above. However, a difficulty arises in effectively extracting common features among these three datasets due to the existence of high variations among them. Therefore, the MAAS-SA method comprises three main components to tackle the difficulty, which include a pretrained transformer as an encoder, a combination of the supervised contrastive, hinge, and focal losses to enhance the feature separability, and an alignment classifier with multiple boundaries based on domain generalization to promote classification ability. Moreover, the alignment classifier of the MAAS-SA method not only extracts common features among these three datasets but also identifies unique features for each dataset. Additionally, a variant of the MAAS-SA method, which is called WMAAS-SA, is developed by integrating wavelet domain signals and Kullback-Leibler divergence (KLD) loss to extract more distinguishable representations and then to improve classification ability on audio anti-spoofing. Finally, experimental results demonstrate that the WMAAS-SA method outperforms existing approaches across datasets, ASVspoof 2019 logical access, Chinese Fake Audio Detection (CFAD), French and Russian language in Multi-Language Audio Anti-Spoofing Dataset (MLAAD). Moreover, it is superior to other schemes on unseen attacks and languages.

title: Array-Aware Ambisonics and HRTF Encoding for Binaural Reproduction With Wearable Arrays
authors: Yhonatan Gayer, Vladimir Tourbabin, Zamir Ben-Hur, David Lou-Alon, Boaz Rafaely
publish: IEEE Transactions on Audio, Speech and Language Processing
abstract: This work introduces a novel method for binaural reproduction from arbitrary microphone arrays, based on array-aware optimization of Ambisonics encoding through Head-Related Transfer Function (HRTF) pre-processing. The proposed approach integrates array-specific information into the HRTF processing pipeline, leading to improved spatial accuracy in binaural rendering. Objective evaluations demonstrate superior performance under simulated wearable-array and head rotations compared to conventional Ambisonics encoding method. A listening experiment further confirms that the method achieves significantly higher perceptual ratings in both timbre and spatial quality. Fully compatible with standard Ambisonics, the proposed method offers a practical solution for spatial audio rendering in applications such as virtual reality, augmented reality, and wearable audio capture.
